DISCOVERY 302, PLAINTIFF KAREN CLOPTON'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET ONE. THE COURT ADOPTS THE TENTATIVE RULING. THE MOTION IS GRANTED AS TO REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION NOS. 39 AND 54. THE MOTION IS GRANTED, IN PART, AS TO RFP NOS. 6, 10, AND 47 AS FOLLOWS: NO. 6 (PLAINTIFF'S JOB PERFORMANCE), THE SCOPE IS NARROWED TO INCLUDE THE INDIVIDUALS RESPONSIBLE FOR EVALUATING PLAINTIFF'S JOB PERFORMANCE, WITHOUT PREJUDICE TO BROADEN THE SCOPE LATER IF THERE IS GOOD CAUSE; NO. 10, THE SCOPE IS NARROWED TO INCLUDE INDIVIDUALS WHO MADE OR APPROVED THE TERMINATION DECISION, WITHOUT PREJUDICE TO BROADEN THE SCOPE LATER IF THERE IS GOOD CAUSE; AND, NO. 47, THE REQUEST IS LIMITED TO AFRICAN-AMERICAN MANAGEMENT-LEVEL EMPLOYEES OF CPUC. THE MOTION IS DENIED AS TO RFP NOS. 3, 11, 14, 16, 18-22, 24, 25, 28, 31-35, 40-46, 48, 50, 51, 57, 58, 60, 63, 64, 69, 72, BASED ON DEFENDANT'S SUPPLEMENTAL RESPONSES INDICATING ALL RESPONSIVE DOCUMENTS HAVE BEEN PRODUCED. THE MOTION IS DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE AS TO NOS. 2, 8, 9, 30, 36, 53. THESE REQUESTS, AS DRAFTED, ARE OVERBROAD, AND PLAINTIFF FILED THE MOTION BEFORE THE PARTIES WERE ABLE TO REACH AGREEMENT HOW TO NARROW THE SCOPE. THE MOTION IS DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE AS TO NO. 49 (SMITH MEMO), NO. 52 (SEMCER EVALUATION), 56 (INVESTIGATION), PENDING DEFENDANT'S PRODUCTION OF A PRIVILEGE LOG. DEFENDANT SHALL PRODUCE A SUFFICIENTLY DETAILED PRIVILEGE LOG IDENTIFYING ANY INFORMATION BEING WITHHELD ON PRIVILEGE OR OTHER GROUNDS. DEFENDANT SHALL SERVE SUPPLEMENTAL VERIFIED RESPONSES, DOCUMENTS AND THE PRIVILEGE LOG WITHIN 30 DAYS. SANCTIONS ARE DENIED. DEFENDANT'S COUNSEL IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER REPEATING VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE COURT'S RULING IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312 BY EMAIL TO THE JUDGE PRO TEM AT AARON@MINNISANDSMALLETS.COM. MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Oct-01-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Dan Siegel, Esq. Jane Brunner, Esq. 510-839-1200 of Siegel Yee & Brunner for Plaintiff.
Suzanne Solomon, Esq., 415-512-3000 of Liebert Cassidy Whitmore for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues and cites a case, reuqests more pages. The Court suggests parties to meet and confer and submit its requests through an ex parte application.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, MICHAEL PICKER, LIANE RANDOLPH'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendants' motion for summary adjudication is denied. Under Code of Civil Procedure 437c(f)(1), "a motion for summary adjudication shall be granted only if it completely disposes of a cause of action." A cause of action has no merit if "a defendant establishes an affirmative defense to that cause of action." (Code Civ. Proc. 437c(o)(2).) A defendant has met that burden if she shows that "there is a complete defense to the cause of action." (Id. 437c(p)(2).)

Defendants here do not meet this burden. They bring their motion based on collateral estoppel, arguing that Plaintiff is "estopped from litigating her termination in this action" by a decision of the State Personnel Board upholding her dismissal. However, the issue of Plaintiff's termination does not comprise any entire cause of action. Rather, termination is but one of 11 separate adverse employment actions on which Plaintiff bases each of her four causes of action. (1st Am. Compl. 31-32, 38-45.) Defendants acknowledge all the other alleged adverse actions in their motion and admit that Plaintiff's allegations do "concern alleged adverse actions aside from the termination," although they attempt to minimize their significance by qualifying them as "a very small portion of Plaintiff's allegations." (Defs.' Memo. at 1-2.) Later, Defendants even state that Plaintiff may maintain her cause of action under the Whistleblower Protection Act (first claim for relief) but is collaterally estopped from basing it on her termination. (Defs.' Memo. at 7.) Defendants fail to establish that they have a complete defense to any of Plaintiff's causes of action. (See McCaskey v. California State Automobile Assn. (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 947, 975 ["If a cause of action is not shown to be barred in its entirety, no order for summary judgment-or adjudication-can be entered."] [trial court erred in granting summary adjudication of FEHA claims on statute of limitations grounds]; Nazir v. United Airlines, Inc. (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 243, 251 & fn. 1 [individual actions that do not completely dispose of an employment discrimination cause of action cannot be summarily adjudicated].)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay Discovery Or In The Alternative For A Protective Order
Off Calendar Jun-05-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION, MICHAEL PICKER, LIANE RANDOLPH's Motion To Stay Discovery Or In The Alternative For A Protective Order.

The court adopts the report and recommendation of the judge pro tem. Defendants California Public Utilities Commission, Michael Picker, and Liane M. Randolph's ill-conceived motion to stay discovery, or in the alternative, for a protective order is denied. Defendants contend that certain of Plaintiff's claims are barred by collateral estoppel or by her failure to exhaust judicial remedies because she did not file a petition for writ of mandate challenging the State Personnel Board's decision upholding her termination. However, the Court will not adjudicate the merits of those contested defenses under the guise of a stay of discovery or a protective order. As Plaintiff correctly argues, "a motion to stay discovery or for a protective order is the wrong motion to argue exhaustion and estoppel." If Defendants wish to raise those issues, they may do so by filing a motion for summary judgment/adjudication as to the purportedly barred claims. Far from being "directly on point," none of the authorities Defendants rely upon supports the relief sought.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar May-29-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
Defendants California Public Utilities Commission, Michael Picker, Carla Peterman, Liane Randolph, Martha Aceves, and Clifford Rechtschaffen's demurrer to the first and third causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Karen Clopton for retaliation in violation of the Whistleblower Protection Act and race discrimination in violation of Government Code 12940 is sustained as to the first cause of action alleged against Ms. Aceves, Ms. Peterman and Mr. Rechtschaffen with twenty days leave to amend and overruled as to all other grounds asserted in the demurrer. While she did so as to Mr. Picker and Ms. Randolph, Ms. Clopton has not sufficiently alleged facts showing that Ms. Aceves, Ms. Peterman and Mr. Rechtschaffen committed a retaliatory act because of a protected disclosure she made. The race discrimination claim is adequately alleged by Ms. Clopton. She alleged that she is a member of a protected class; she performed competently in the position she held; she suffered adverse employment actions culminating in her termination; there are circumstances suggesting discriminatory motive (the hiring of a trainer over the complaints of Ms. Clopton that he made racists remarks and the statement "Are you ready to meet with three white men with white hair?"). Order signed. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR #8600 =302/hk
